content_preview.title: Valkey Turns Two: How a Linux Foundation Fork Became the Open-Source Redis Successor
content_preview.summary: Eighteen months after Redis changed its license, Valkey 9.0 has reached one billion requests per second, 346 contributors, and 75 percent of surveyed Redis users eyeing migration.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

In March 2024, Redis Inc. abruptly relicensed its flagship in-memory data store, abandoning the permissive BSD 3-clause license in favor of a dual RSALv2/SSPLv1 model that effectively closed the door on cloud-hosted deployments by competing providers. Within days, Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, Oracle, Alibaba, Ericsson, and others had convened under the Linux Foundation to fork the codebase under a project called Valkey.

Two years on, that emergency fork has matured into a fea...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured Analysis at 1,589 words. Comprehensive coverage of the Valkey story: licensing context, 8.x foundation work, 9.0 architectural advances, community trajectory, adoption data, and open questions. The 'What Remains Unknown' section is a notable editorial strength. Round 2 rewrite is stronger than the original — the 8.1 section now carries richer technical detail and correct source attribution.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 9 sources verified. The two new sources added in the rewrite (valkey.io/blog/valkey-8-1-0-ga/ and valkey.io/blog/new-hash-table/) directly and explicitly support the disputed statistics. The Valkey 8.1 GA blog states verbatim: 'This change improves the rate of accepting new connections by around 300%.' The new hash table blog states: 'we've managed to reduce the memory usage by roughly 20 bytes per key-value pair' and 'roughly 30 bytes per key-value pair' for keys with TTL. Both figures are now correctly attributed. The allowlist warning is a system artifact — valkey.io, linuxfoundation.org, and phoronix.com are all authoritative, directly relevant primary sources.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified against cited sources. Round 1 findings fully resolved: (1) the ~300% TLS connection acceptance rate improvement is explicitly stated in valkey.io/blog/valkey-8-1-0-ga/ and supported by GitHub PR #1338 benchmark data; (2) the 20-30 bytes per-key overhead reduction is documented verbatim in valkey.io/blog/new-hash-table/. All other figures verified in Round 1 remain accurate and unmodified.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. The Analysis section contextualizes findings without editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: No previous Valkey or Redis fork articles in publication archives. Genuinely new topic.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Round 2 submission fully addresses both Round 1 findings. The bot correctly identified that both disputed statistics were real and documentable from primary Valkey engineering sources, and resolved the issue by adding proper citations rather than removing accurate figures. The article is stronger for it. Ready for publication.
